ground_truth.title: The best budget smartphone you can buy
ground_truth.author: Allison Johnson
ground_truth.main_content:
You can’t get everything for under $600, but you can get a great smartphone. Whether you want an iPhone, a 120Hz screen, or water resistance, you’ve got options.

Some of us take a kind of “I eat to live” rather than an “I live to eat” approach to gadgets. They’re tools that help you get things done, not something you want to invest a lot of time or money in. If that’s you — and there’s no judgment here from a certifiable gadget nerd — then you can probably think of more worthwhile ways to spend $1,000 than on a phone.

Budget phones to the rescue. These devices are roughly $500 or under, though not all of them, and they’re more capable than ever. You won’t get all the bells and whistles, but you will save a little money to spend on, I don’t know, actual bells and whistles. It’s your world.

What compromises can you expect from a budget phone? Some combination of the following: slower processors, less storage, and worse cameras than flagship phones, almost across the board. Many have lower-resolution screens, and water resistance is often less robust than on a pricier phone.

What I’m looking for

How we test
There’s no shortcut to properly testing a phone; I put my personal SIM card (physical or otherwise) in each phone I review and live with it for a minimum of one full week. I set up each phone from scratch, load it up with my apps, and go about living my life — stress testing the battery, using GPS navigation on my bike while streaming radio, taking rapid-fire portrait mode photos of my kid — everything I can throw at it. Starting over with a new phone every week either sounds like a dream or your personal hell, depending on how Into Phones you are. For me, switching has become so routine that it’s mostly painless.

Strong software support
At least a couple of years of OS upgrades and, ideally, three years of security updates. There’s no point in buying even a cheap phone if you have to replace it after just a couple of years because it stopped getting security patches.

A good screen
Since you look at it roughly two thousand times a day, your phone’s screen is one place you shouldn’t compromise. An OLED has richer contrast and color than an LCD, and the big screens on today’s phones really need at least a 1080p resolution. Faster refresh rates like 90Hz and even 120Hz are becoming more common on budget phone screens; however, for my money, a smooth-scrolling LCD doesn’t look as nice as an OLED with a standard refresh rate.

Serviceable storage space
If you plan to hang onto your phone for a while, you’ll want enough storage space to accommodate all the system files, photos, and videos you’ll accumulate over the years. Ideally, you’ll get at least 128GB built in.

One good rear camera, not four mediocre ones
Upgrades like telephoto cameras and optical image stabilization are rare in the under-$500 class, but you can still expect good, basic performance in good lighting from any modern smartphone. Low light is trickier. Phones in this class should offer a night mode to help with non-moving subjects in very dim light. And there are no bonus points awarded for adding extra macro and depth cameras to pad out the rear camera array — those 2- and 5-megapixel sensors are pretty much useless.

The best cheap iPhone

Screen: 6.1-inch, 1170p OLED / Processor: A18 Cameras: 48-megapixel f/1.6 with OIS, 12-megapixel selfie / Charging: 20W wired, 7.5W wireless / Weather-resistance rating: IP68

If I were making this recommendation to you face-to-face, you’d hear a heavy sigh. Yes, this is the cheapest new iPhone Apple makes. Yes, it comes with most of the things that make an iPhone an iPhone. But it comes with some significant tradeoffs — some of which make more sense than others — and it’s not exactly cheap. If you’re amenable to last year’s model or a refurbished iPhone 14, one of those might actually be a better option. But for an unfussy person who just wants a new iPhone for the least amount of money, the 16E will do the trick.

There’s a single 48-megapixel rear camera on the back, meaning there’s no ultrawide like on the newer iPhone Air. That’s an understandable tradeoff — so is the use of the older “notch” design rather than the Dynamic Island. But it’s harder to understand why Apple left out MagSafe here — that’s the ring of magnets built into the back of virtually every other iPhone since 2020. The 16E still supports wireless charging, but it can’t take advantage of the ecosystem of magnetic chargers and accessories on its own; you’ll need to add a magnetic case. This is a silly omission, and Apple should feel bad about it.

Another heavy sigh: the 16E supports Apple Intelligence, which you won’t get if you opt for an iPhone 15 or 14. Should you care? It’s really hard to say. What exists of Apple Intelligence so far is underwhelming, and the most interesting bits won’t arrive anytime soon. If you want to future-proof your purchase as much as possible, the 16E will be ready for Apple’s AI. But don’t buy one expecting a life-changing experience now. It’s just an iPhone after all, for better and worse, and right now it’s the best price you’ll find on a brand-new one.

Read our full iPhone 16E review.

The best cheap Android phone

Screen: 6.3-inch, 1080p OLED, 120Hz / Processor: Tensor G4 Cameras: 48-megapixel f/1.7 with OIS, 13-megapixel ultrawide, 13-megapixel selfie / Battery: 5,100mAh / Charging: 23W wired, 7.5W wireless / Weather-resistance rating: IP68

Google’s Pixel A-series phones have been my go-to recommendation for a cheap Android phone for years, but there was still room for improvement. With the 9A, Google made some modest tweaks that make it even easier to recommend — and at $499, the price is right.

The phone now has an IP68 rating, meaning it’s fully dust-tight and can survive a dunk in water. That’s a big upgrade over the IP67 rating on the 8A, which was only rated for a meter of water for 30 minutes. The 9A’s screen is a little bigger and a little brighter, and it’s a 120Hz OLED, which is a nice upgrade over the 90Hz screen on the 8A.

The camera system is the same as the 8A’s, which is to say it’s very good for the price. The 48-megapixel main camera takes great photos in good light, and the 13-megapixel ultrawide is a nice addition. The 13-megapixel selfie camera is also very good.

The 9A is powered by Google’s Tensor G4 chip, which is plenty fast for everyday tasks. It also has 8GB of RAM and 128GB of storage, which is a good amount for most people. The battery life is also very good, thanks to the 5,100mAh battery.

The 9A runs Android 14 with Google’s Pixel UI on top. It’s a clean, simple interface that’s easy to use. Google also promises seven years of software updates, which is a huge plus.

The only real downside to the 9A is that it doesn’t have wireless charging. That’s a bummer, but it’s not a deal-breaker for most people.

Overall, the Pixel 9A is the best cheap Android phone you can buy. It has a great screen, a great camera, and great software support. It’s also water-resistant and has a big battery. If you’re looking for a cheap Android phone, this is the one to get.

Read our full Pixel 9A review.

The budget phone with a big, beautiful screen

Screen: 6.7-inch, 1080p OLED, 120Hz / Processor: Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 Cameras: 50-megapixel f/1.8 with OIS, 8-megapixel ultrawide, 16-megapixel selfie / Battery: 5,500mAh / Charging: 100W wired, 50W wireless / Weather-resistance rating: IP65

If you want a big, beautiful screen on a budget, the OnePlus 13R is the phone to get. It has a 6.7-inch OLED display with a 120Hz refresh rate, and it’s one of the best screens you’ll find on a phone in this price range.

The 13R is powered by Qualcomm’s Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 chip, which is plenty fast for everyday tasks. It also has 12GB of RAM and 256GB of storage, which is a lot for a phone in this price range.

The camera system is good, but not great. The 50-megapixel main camera takes good photos in good light, but it struggles in low light. The 8-megapixel ultrawide is also just okay. The 16-megapixel selfie camera is good.

The battery life is excellent, thanks to the 5,500mAh battery. And the 100W wired charging is incredibly fast. The 13R also supports 50W wireless charging, which is a nice bonus.

The 13R runs Android 14 with OnePlus’ OxygenOS on top. It’s a clean, simple interface that’s easy to use. OnePlus also promises three years of software updates and four years of security updates, which is good.

The only real downside to the 13R is that it’s not water-resistant. It has an IP65 rating, which means it’s protected from dust and low-pressure water jets, but it’s not fully waterproof.

Overall, the OnePlus 13R is a great phone for the price. It has a big, beautiful screen, a fast processor, and a big battery with fast charging. If you want a big-screen phone on a budget, this is the one to get.

Read our full OnePlus 13R review.

Other budget phones to consider

If you’re looking for a cheap phone with a great camera, the Google Pixel 8A is a good option. It has the same camera system as the Pixel 9A, but it’s a little cheaper. It also has a 90Hz OLED display and an IP67 rating.

If you’re looking for a cheap phone with a great battery, the Samsung Galaxy A35 is a good option. It has a 5,000mAh battery that will easily last you all day. It also has a 6.6-inch OLED display and an IP67 rating.

If you’re looking for a cheap phone with a great screen, the Motorola Edge 40 Neo is a good option. It has a 6.55-inch OLED display with a 144Hz refresh rate. It also has a 5,000mAh battery and an IP68 rating.

What’s coming next

We’re expecting to see new budget phones from Google, Samsung, and OnePlus in the coming months. Google’s Pixel 10A is expected to launch in the fall, and it’s rumored to have a new design and a better camera. Samsung’s Galaxy A55 is expected to launch in the spring, and it’s rumored to have a better processor and a better camera. OnePlus’ 14R is expected to launch in the spring, and it’s rumored to have a better screen and a better camera.

We’ll update this guide as new phones are released.
